# Chapter NN — Topic

Previous chapter · [Handbook](../README.md) · Next chapter

Replace the plain previous/next labels with valid relative links when creating a chapter.

> **Learning objectives**
> - Objective one.
> - Objective two.
> - Objective three.

## 1. Introduction

Define the topic, explain why it matters, list prerequisites, and state what the reader will be able to do.

## 2. Theory

Explain vocabulary, behavior, protocol fields, standards, and boundaries. Prefer small tables over dense paragraphs.

### Did you know?

Add one accurate, useful fact—not trivia for its own sake.

### Memory trick

Provide a mnemonic only when it reinforces the correct model.

### Behind the scenes

Describe implementation details or behavior hidden by normal tools.

## 3. Visual diagram

Use Mermaid for flows and relationships. Keep labels short and explain the diagram in prose.

## 4. Real-world example

Connect the concept to a realistic user or infrastructure action.

### Real industry usage

Explain where teams use this knowledge in production.

### Cloud perspective

Map the concept to cloud primitives without implying that every provider behaves identically.

### DevOps perspective

Show how the concept affects deployment, observability, reliability, or automation.

### Cybersecurity perspective

Describe relevant threats, controls, and evidence without providing unsafe instructions.

## 5. Packet journey

Walk from source to destination. State what changes at each hop and what remains end-to-end.

## 6. Linux commands

| Command | What it does | When to use it |
|---|---|---|
| `command` | Purpose | Situation |

For each important command include a tested example and explain meaningful output fields.

## 7. Practical example

Give a reproducible example with objective, prerequisites, steps, expected observations, cleanup, and safety notes.

## 8. Wireshark example

Include a display filter, capture context, packet sequence, and important fields. Never publish credentials, tokens, public IPs, or unrelated traffic.

## 9. Common mistakes

Include both conceptual errors and common beginner mistakes. Explain why each is wrong.

## 10. Troubleshooting

Start from the symptom, gather evidence, isolate the layer, and confirm the fix. Do not present random command lists.

### Best practices

List practices that are specific, testable, and relevant to this topic.

## 11. Interview questions

Mix definition, comparison, troubleshooting, and scenario questions. Put answers in collapsible sections.

## 12. Quiz

Include multiple choice, true/false, practical, and scenario questions. Put answers after all questions.

## FAQ

Answer questions a careful beginner is likely to ask.

## 13. Summary

Summarize the mental model and link to the next chapter and related lab.
